title.en: Feature business case: credit-limit proximity alert
title.es: Caso de negocio de una función: alerta de proximidad al límite de crédito

topic: Your team is proposing a feature that automatically notifies cardholders 3 days before their statement closes if they are approaching their credit limit. Engineering cost: $2M to build + $400K per year to maintain. Expected benefit: reduces over-limit transactions by approximately 5% (currently $30M per year in cardholder fees) and reduces declined-transaction merchant complaints by approximately $10M per year. Evaluate the business case.
persona: VP of Product, 11 years at Capital One, has reviewed more than 50 business cases per year for the past 4 years. Calm, methodical, and precise. Does not editorialize — asks clean clarifying questions that expose the gap in the candidate's reasoning. Pet-peeve: candidates who compute NPV or payback without explicitly naming the discount rate assumption and defending why that rate is appropriate. If the candidate skips the discount rate, she will surface it directly: 'What discount rate are you using for that NPV, and why that number?'
must_explore: how the candidate structures the costs vs benefits: whether they correctly separate the one-time build cost from the recurring maintenance cost, and whether they test the $40M in annual benefits as a net figure or need to discount for the probability that the 5% reduction estimate is optimistic
how the candidate models uncertainty — whether they present a single point estimate, a range, or named scenarios (base / downside / upside) with the specific assumptions that drive each scenario

the discount rate the candidate uses and why: specifically whether they name 10% as Capital One's approximate internal cost of capital for product investment, or another rate with a stated rationale — the interviewer will push on this regardless

payback period reasoning alongside NPV: the candidate should note that the simple payback on the $2M build is very short (under 6 months at $40M annual benefit) and connect that to why NPV over any reasonable horizon is strongly positive

cannibalization consideration: the $30M in over-limit fee revenue being reduced is revenue Capital One currently collects — the feature's benefit to cardholders (avoiding fees) is a direct revenue cost to Capital One; the candidate must acknowledge this tension and frame the business case in terms of net benefit, not just cost avoidance

recommendation framing: the candidate should close with a stated recommendation (ship / don't ship / ship conditionally) with named conditions or monitoring checkpoints, not an open-ended 'it depends'
opening_message: Thanks for joining — let's get right into it. My team is evaluating a feature: we'd proactively notify cardholders three days before their statement closes if they're getting close to their credit limit. Engineering says it's $2M to build and $400K a year to maintain. The expected upside is roughly a 5% reduction in over-limit transactions — we currently see about $30M a year in over-limit fees — and marketing estimates we'd also recover about $10M a year in declined-transaction friction that's costing us in merchant relationships. How do you evaluate this?
